bfbtools 0.1.0
  • Reference
  • Articles
    • The bfbtools Algorithms

    The bfbtools Algorithms

    bfbtools-algorithms.Rmd
    library(bfbtools)

    bfbtools is deliberately sim-agnostic: it has no dependency on bfbgill or bridges, and works on plain integer count-vectors. This vignette covers its three functions on their own. If you’re working with real cna_data tibbles from simulations or single-cell sequencing, see bridges::bfb_detect_batch() (vignette("batch-detection", package = "bridges")) for the batch/tibble-aware wrapper built on top of what’s shown here.

    1 Background: what is a count-vector?

    A chromosome (or one arm of it) that has undergone some number of breakage-fusion-bridge cycles has a copy-number profile that, read from telomere to centromere, forms a nested palindromic structure. Collapsing consecutive equal-copy-number segments gives a count-vector: an ordered sequence of positive integers, one per distinct segment, e.g. c(6, 3, 5).

    Not every count-vector can arise from a BFB schedule – deciding whether one can is a genuine combinatorial question, and the naive way to answer it (searching over all possible schedules) is worst-case exponential in the vector’s length. bfbtools implements the linear-time algorithm of Zakov, Kinsella & Bafna (2013, PNAS), which answers this in time proportional to the vector’s length, with no search budget or backtracking ever needed.

    2 admits_bfb() – does this count-vector admit a BFB schedule? 

    admits_bfb(c(6, 3, 5))               # TRUE
#> [1] TRUE
admits_bfb(c(14, 7, 18, 16, 9, 12))  # FALSE -- a real near-miss example
#> [1] FALSE

    This is a genuine yes/no combinatorial fact about the vector, computed exactly – not an approximation or a statistical test. admits_bfb() always returns a definitive TRUE/FALSE; unlike the earlier exponential-search algorithms this package’s linear-time implementation supersedes, it never needs a search-step budget or times out.

    A precondition worth knowing about: trivial vectors 

    admits_bfb(c(1, 1))
#> [1] TRUE

    This returns TRUE – but not because the vector shows any real BFB signal. “Admits a BFB schedule” is true even for a schedule with zero breakage-fusion-bridge cycles: the untouched baseline chromosome is itself a (trivial) BFB schedule, in the same sense that “4 is even” is true without 4 having done anything to become so. Since every real BFB cycle is an inverted duplication, it necessarily pushes at least one segment’s copy number above 1 – so a vector where every segment sits at copy number 1 (e.g. a flat region interrupted only by a plain deletion) is definitionally uninteresting for this question, even though it technically “admits BFB”. If you’re screening many vectors and want to exclude this case, check max(v) > 1 alongside admits_bfb(v) (this is exactly what bridges::bfb_detect_batch()’s min_max_count argument does for you automatically).

    3 nearest_bfb() – how far is a noisy vector from admitting BFB?

    Real copy-number calls are noisy. nearest_bfb() finds the highest-weight BFB-admitting count-vector explaining an observed, potentially noisy vector, under a Poisson error model (observed counts modeled as noisy draws around the true count):

    r <- nearest_bfb(c(14, 7, 18, 16, 9, 12))
r
#> $weight
#> [1] 0.9114639
#> 
#> $vector
#> [1] 14  7 19 17  9 13

    weight is in (0, 1]: 1 means the vector already admits BFB exactly (no correction needed); values closer to 0 mean the nearest BFB-admitting vector required larger or more numerous corrections. Because it’s a product of per-position probabilities, weight mechanically shrinks as the vector gets longer even when each position matches equally well – it’s a useful number to eyeball for one vector at a time, but not for comparing vectors of different lengths.

    bfb_distance_weighted(c(14, 7, 18, 16, 9, 12))
#> [1] 0.09270324

    bfb_distance_weighted() is -log(weight): 0 for an exact match, and (unlike weight) a sum rather than a product across positions – making it the better statistic to compare across vectors of different lengths, or to use as a response variable in downstream models.

    Both functions accept model = "none" to require an exact match only (equivalent to admits_bfb(), useful mainly for testing), and a min_weight cutoff that bounds how far the search looks before giving up (returning NULL if nothing is found).

    4 A note on performance and licensing

    admits_bfb() and nearest_bfb() are both linear-time-core algorithms: even on inputs that make exponential-search approaches take many seconds (or fail to terminate within a reasonable budget), these resolve in milliseconds, regardless of how “hard” the specific instance is. There is no search-step budget to tune for correctness – only nearest_bfb()’s max_frontier safety cap, which exists purely to catch genuinely pathological inputs gracefully (returning NA with a warning) rather than growing memory use unboundedly.

    bfbtools is GPL-3 licensed, as a derivative of the reference implementation these algorithms are ported from (Zakov, Kinsella & Bafna’s own Java implementation). This is why it’s kept as an independent package rather than folded into bridges (MIT licensed) – depending on a GPL-3 package is fine and doesn’t affect bridges’s own license, but merging the source would.

    Developed by Giovanni Santacatterina.

    Site built with pkgdown 2.2.0.
